elements.sent-2411-006: These instructions give you guidelines for preparing papers for IEEE Access.
elements.sent-2411-007: Use this document as a template if you are using \LaTeX.
elements.sent-2411-008: Otherwise, use this document as an instruction set.

elements.sent-2411-010: Paper titles should be written in uppercase and lowercase letters, not all uppercase.

elements.sent-2411-015: The abstract must be a concise yet comprehensive reflection of what is in your article.
elements.sent-2411-016: In particular, the abstract must be self-contained, without abbreviations, footnotes, or references.
elements.sent-2411-017: It should be a microcosm of the full article.
elements.sent-2411-018: The abstract must be between 150--250 words.
elements.sent-2411-019: Be sure that you adhere to these limits; otherwise, you will need to edit your abstract accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-020: The abstract must be written as one paragraph, and should not contain displayed mathematical equations or tabular material.
elements.sent-2411-021: The abstract should include three or four different keywords or phrases, as this will help readers to find it.
elements.sent-2411-022: It is important to avoid over-repetition of such phrases as this can result in a page being rejected by search engines.
elements.sent-2411-023: Ensure that your abstract reads well and is grammatically correct.

A graph within a graph is an ``inset,'' not an ``insert.'' The word ``alternatively'' is preferred to the word ``alternately'' (unless you really mean something that alternates).

elements.sent-2411-075: Do not confuse ``imply'' and ``infer.'' Prefixes such as ``non,'' ``sub,'' ``micro,'' ``multi,'' and ``ultra'' are not independent words; they should be joined to the words they modify, usually without a hyphen.

elements.sent-2411-083: Figures compiled of more than one sub-figure presented side-by-side, or stacked.
elements.sent-2411-084: If a multipart figure is made up of multiple figure types (one part is lineart, and another is grayscale or color) the figure should meet the stricter guidelines.
elements.sent-2411-085: Format and save your graphics using a suitable graphics processing program that will allow you to create the images as PostScript (PS), Encapsulated PostScript (.EPS), Tagged Image File Format (.TIFF), Portable Document Format (.PDF), Portable Network Graphics (.PNG), or Metapost (.MPS), sizes them, and adjusts the resolution settings.
elements.sent-2411-086: When submitting your final paper, your graphics should all be submitted individually in one of these formats along with the manuscript.
elements.sent-2411-087: Most charts, graphs, and tables are one column wide (3.5 inches/88 millimeters/21 picas) or page wide (7.16 inches/181 millimeters/43 picas).
elements.sent-2411-088: The maximum depth a graphic can be is 8.5 inches (216 millimeters/54 picas).
elements.sent-2411-089: When choosing the depth of a graphic, please allow space for a caption.
elements.sent-2411-090: Figures can be sized between column and page widths if the author chooses, however it is recommended that figures are not sized less than column width unless when necessary.
elements.sent-2411-091: There is currently one publication with column measurements that do not coincide with those listed above.
elements.sent-2411-092: Proceedings of the IEEE has a column measurement of 3.25 inches (82.5 millimeters/19.5 picas).
elements.sent-2411-093: The final printed size of author photographs is exactly 1 inch wide by 1.25 inches tall (25.4 millimeters$\,\times\,$31.75 millimeters/6 picas$\,\times\,$7.5 picas).
elements.sent-2411-094: Author photos printed in editorials measure 1.59 inches wide by 2 inches tall (40 millimeters$\,\times\,$50 millimeters/9.5 picas$\,\times\,$12 picas).
elements.sent-2411-095: The proper resolution of your figures will depend on the type of figure it is as defined in the ``Types of Figures'' section.
elements.sent-2411-096: Author photographs, color, and grayscale figures should be at least 300dpi.
elements.sent-2411-097: Line art, including tables should be a minimum of 600dpi.
elements.sent-2411-098: In order to preserve the figures' integrity across multiple computer platforms, we accept files in the following formats: .EPS/.PDF/.PS.
elements.sent-2411-099: All fonts must be embedded or text converted to outlines in order to achieve the best-quality results.
elements.sent-2411-100: The term color space refers to the entire sum of colors that can be represented within the said medium.
elements.sent-2411-101: For our purposes, the three main color spaces are Grayscale, RGB (red/green/blue) and CMYK (cyan/magenta/yellow/black).
elements.sent-2411-102: RGB is generally used with on-screen graphics, whereas CMYK is used for printing purposes.
elements.sent-2411-103: All color figures should be generated in RGB or CMYK color space.
elements.sent-2411-104: Grayscale images should be submitted in Grayscale color space.
elements.sent-2411-105: Line art may be provided in grayscale OR bitmap colorspace.
elements.sent-2411-106: Note that ``bitmap colorspace'' and ``bitmap file format'' are not the same thing.
elements.sent-2411-107: When bitmap color space is selected, .TIF/.TIFF/.PNG are the recommended file formats.
elements.sent-2411-108: When preparing your graphics IEEE suggests that you use of one of the following Open Type fonts: Times New Roman, Helvetica, Arial, Cambria, and Symbol.
elements.sent-2411-109: If you are supplying EPS, PS, or PDF files all fonts must be embedded.
elements.sent-2411-110: Some fonts may only be native to your operating system; without the fonts embedded, parts of the graphic may be distorted or missing.
elements.sent-2411-111: A safe option when finalizing your figures is to strip out the fonts before you save the files, creating ``outline'' type.
elements.sent-2411-112: This converts fonts to artwork what will appear uniformly on any screen.
elements.sent-2411-113: Figure axis labels are often a source of confusion.
elements.sent-2411-115: As an example, write the quantity ``Magnetization,'' or ``Magnetization M,'' not just ``M.'' Put units in parentheses.
elements.sent-2411-116: Do not label axes only with units.
elements.sent-2411-117: As in Fig. 1, for example, write ``Magnetization (A/m)'' or ``Magnetization (A$\cdot$m$^{-1}$),'' not just ``A/m.'' Do not label axes with a ratio of quantities and units.
elements.sent-2411-118: For example, write ``Temperature (K),'' not ``Temperature/K.'' Multipliers can be especially confusing.
elements.sent-2411-119: Write ``Magnetization (kA/m)'' or ``Magnetization (10$^{3}$ A/m).'' Do not write ``Magnetization (A/m)$\,\times\,$1000'' because the reader would not know whether the top axis label in Fig. 1 meant 16000 A/m or 0.016 A/m.
elements.sent-2411-120: Figure labels should be legible, approximately 8 to 10 point type.
elements.subsubsection-2411-006: Subfigure Labels in Multipart Figures and Tables
elements.sent-2411-121: Multipart figures should be combined and labeled before final submission.
elements.sent-2411-122: Labels should appear centered below each subfigure in 8 point Times New Roman font in the format of (a) (b) (c).
elements.sent-2411-123: Figures (line artwork or photographs) should be named starting with the first 5 letters of the author's last name.
elements.sent-2411-124: The next characters in the filename should be the number that represents the sequential location of this image in your article.
elements.sent-2411-125: For example, in author ``Anderson's'' paper, the first three figures would be named ander1.tif, ander2.tif, and ander3.ps.
elements.sent-2411-126: Tables should contain only the body of the table (not the caption) and should be named similarly to figures, except that `.t' is inserted in-between the author's name and the table number.
elements.sent-2411-127: For example, author Anderson's first three tables would be named ander.t1.tif, ander.t2.ps, ander.t3.eps.
elements.sent-2411-128: Author photographs should be named using the first five characters of the pictured author's last name.
elements.sent-2411-129: For example, four author photographs for a paper may be named: oppen.ps, moshc.tif, chen.eps, and duran.pdf.
elements.sent-2411-130: If two authors or more have the same last name, their first initial(s) can be substituted for the fifth, fourth, third$\ldots$ letters of their surname until the degree where there is differentiation.
elements.sent-2411-131: For example, two authors Michael and Monica Oppenheimer's photos would be named oppmi.tif, and oppmo.eps.
elements.subsection-2411-015: Referencing a Figure or Table Within Your Paper
elements.sent-2411-132: When referencing your figures and tables within your paper, use the abbreviation ``Fig.'' even at the beginning of a sentence.
elements.sent-2411-133: Do not abbreviate ``Table.'' Tables should be numbered with Roman Numerals.
elements.subsection-2411-016: Checking Your Figures: The IEEE Graphics Analyzer
elements.sent-2411-134: The IEEE Graphics Analyzer enables authors to pre-screen their graphics for compliance with IEEE Access standards before submission.
elements.sent-2411-135: The online tool, located at http://graphicsqc.ieee.org/, allows authors to upload their graphics in order to check that each file is the correct file format, resolution, size and colorspace; that no fonts are missing or corrupt; that figures are not compiled in layers or have transparency, and that they are named according to the IEEE Access naming convention.
elements.sent-2411-136: At the end of this automated process, authors are provided with a detailed report on each graphic within the web applet, as well as by email.
elements.sent-2411-137: For more information on using the Graphics Analyzer or any other graphics related topic, contact the IEEE Graphics Help Desk by e-mail at graphics@ieee.org.
elements.sent-2411-138: Because IEEE will do the final formatting of your paper, you do not need to position figures and tables at the top and bottom of each column.
elements.sent-2411-139: In fact, all figures, figure captions, and tables can be placed at the end of your paper.
elements.sent-2411-140: In addition to, or even in lieu of submitting figures within your final manuscript, figures should be submitted individually, separate from the manuscript in one of the file formats listed above in Section \ref{formats .}
elements.sent-2411-141: Place figure captions below the figures; place table titles above the tables.
elements.sent-2411-142: Please do not include captions as part of the figures, or put them in ``text boxes'' linked to the figures.
elements.sent-2411-143: Also, do not place borders around the outside of your figures.
elements.sent-2411-144: All IEEE Transactions, Journals, and Letters allow an author to publish color figures on IEEE Xplore\textregistered\ at no charge, and automatically convert them to grayscale for print versions.
elements.sent-2411-145: In most journals, figures and tables may alternatively be printed in color if an author chooses to do so.
elements.sent-2411-146: Please note that this service comes at an extra expense to the author.
elements.sent-2411-147: If you intend to have print color graphics, include a note with your final paper indicating which figures or tables you would like to be handled that way, and stating that you are willing to pay the additional fee.
elements.sent-2411-148: A conclusion section is not required.
elements.sent-2411-149: Although a conclusion may review the main points of the paper, do not replicate the abstract as the conclusion.
elements.sent-2411-150: A conclusion might elaborate on the importance of the work or suggest applications and extensions.
elements.sent-2411-151: The preferred spelling of the word ``acknowledgment'' in American English is without an ``e'' after the ``g.'' Use the singular heading even if you have many acknowledgments.

elements.sent-2411-155: Multiple references are each numbered with separate brackets.
elements.sent-2411-156: When citing a section in a book, please give the relevant page numbers.
elements.sent-2411-157: In text, refer simply to the reference number.
elements.sent-2411-158: Do not use ``Ref.'' or ``reference'' except at the beginning of a sentence: ``Reference \cite{b3 shows $\ldots$ .'' Please do not use automatic endnotes in Word, rather, type the reference list at the end of the paper using the ``References'' style.}

elements.sent-2411-162: Use them all; use et al. only if names are not given.
elements.sent-2411-163: Use commas around Jr., Sr., and III in names.. Abbreviate conference titles.
elements.sent-2411-164: When citing IEEE transactions, provide the issue number, page range, volume number, year, and/or month if available.
elements.sent-2411-165: When referencing a patent, provide the day and the month of issue, or application.
elements.sent-2411-166: References may not include all information; please obtain and include relevant information.
elements.sent-2411-168: There must be only one reference with each number.
elements.sent-2411-169: If there is a URL included with the print reference, it can be included at the end of the reference.
elements.sent-2411-170: Other than books, capitalize only the first word in a paper title, except for proper nouns and element symbols.
elements.sent-2411-171: For papers published in translation journals, please give the English citation first, followed by the original foreign-language citation See the end of this document for formats and examples of common references.
elements.sent-2411-172: For a complete discussion of references and their formats, see the IEEE style manual at http://www.ieee.org/authortools.
elements.sent-2411-173: Number footnotes separately in superscript numbers.\footnote{It is recommended that footnotes be avoided (except for the unnumbered footnote with the receipt date on the first page).}
elements.sent-2411-174: Instead, try to integrate the footnote information into the text.

elements.sent-2411-176: Use letters for table footnotes (see Table \ref{table ).}
elements.sent-2411-177: When you submit your final version (after your paper has been accepted), print it in two-column format, including figures and tables.
elements.sent-2411-178: You must also send your final manuscript on a disk, via e-mail, or through a Web manuscript submission system as directed by the society contact.
elements.sent-2411-179: You may use Zip for large files, or compress files using Compress, Pkzip, Stuffit, or Gzip.

elements.sent-2411-182: This information will be used to send each author a complimentary copy of the journal in which the paper appears.
elements.sent-2411-183: In addition, designate one author as the ``corresponding author.'' This is the author to whom proofs of the paper will be sent.
elements.sent-2411-184: Proofs are sent to the corresponding author only.
elements.sent-2411-185: Contributions to the Transactions, Journals, and Letters may be submitted electronically on IEEE's on-line manuscript submission and peer-review system, ScholarOne\textregistered\ Manuscripts.
elements.sent-2411-186: You can get a listing of the publications that participate in ScholarOne at http://www.ieee.org/publications\_standards/publications/\breakauthors/authors\_submission.html.

elements.sent-2411-220: Authors should consider the following points:.
elements.sent-2411-221: Technical papers submitted for publication must advance the state of knowledge and must cite relevant prior work.
elements.sent-2411-222: The length of a submitted paper should be commensurate with the importance, or appropriate to the complexity, of the work.
elements.sent-2411-223: For example, an obvious extension of previously published work might not be appropriate for publication or might be adequately treated in just a few pages.
elements.sent-2411-224: Authors must convince both peer reviewers and the editors of the scientific and technical merit of a paper; the standards of proof are higher when extraordinary or unexpected results are reported.
elements.sent-2411-225: Because replication is required for scientific progress, papers submitted for publication must provide sufficient information to allow readers to perform similar experiments or calculations and use the reported results.
elements.sent-2411-226: Although not everything need be disclosed, a paper must contain new, useable, and fully described information.
elements.sent-2411-227: For example, a specimen's chemical composition need not be reported if the main purpose of a paper is to introduce a new measurement technique.
elements.sent-2411-228: Authors should expect to be challenged by reviewers if the results are not supported by adequate data and critical details.
elements.sent-2411-229: Papers that describe ongoing work or announce the latest technical achievement, which are suitable for presentation at a professional conference, may not be appropriate for publication.

elements.sent-2411-317: Visual question answering(VQA) refers to the problem where given an image and a natural language question about the image, a correct natural language answer has to be generated.
elements.sent-2411-318: A VQA model has to demonstrate both the visual understanding of the image and the semantic understanding of the question.
elements.sent-2411-319: It also has to possess the necessary reasoning capability to deduce the answer correctly.
elements.sent-2411-320: Consequently, a capable VQA model would lead to significant advancements in artificial intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-321: Since the inception of this field, a plethora of VQA datasets and models have been published.
elements.sent-2411-322: In this article, we meticulously analyze the current state of VQA datasets and models while cleanly dividing them into distinct categories and then summarize the methodologies and characteristics of each category.
elements.sent-2411-323: We divide VQA datasets into four categories:.
elements.sent-2411-324: Available datasets that contain a rich collection of authentic images.
elements.sent-2411-325: Synthetic datasets that contain only synthetic images produced through artificial means.
elements.sent-2411-326: Diagnostic datasets that are specially designed to test model performance in a particular area, e.g., understanding the scene text.
elements.sent-2411-327: KB (Knowledge-Based) datasets that are designed to measure a model's ability to utilize outside knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-328: Concurrently, we explore six main paradigms of VQA models: Fusion is where we discuss different methods of fusing information between visual and textual modalities.
elements.sent-2411-329: Attention is the technique of using information from one modality to filter information from another.
elements.sent-2411-330: External knowledge base where we discuss different models utilizing outside information.
elements.sent-2411-331: Composition or Reasoning, where we analyze techniques to answer advanced questions that require complex reasoning steps.
elements.sent-2411-332: Explanation, which is the process of generating visual and/or textual descriptions to verify sound Reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-333: Graph models which encode and manipulate relationships through nodes in a graph.
elements.sent-2411-334: We also discuss some miscellaneous topics, such as scene text understanding, counting, and bias reduction.
elements.sent-2411-335: The field of computer vision has seen significant advances in recent times.
elements.sent-2411-336: Since the development of neural networks, many computer vision problems such as captioning, object recognition, object detection, and scene classification have seen tremendous progress.
elements.sent-2411-337: Moreover, the advent of social networking sites, especially image-sharing websites, has led to abundant image data.
elements.sent-2411-338: Many efficient methods have been developed to extract and collect this data, making large-scale image datasets possible.
elements.sent-2411-339: Crowd-sourcing has also made it possible to quickly utilize human workers to perform captioning, annotation, etc. The same is true for natural language processing, another field to which VQA belongs.
elements.sent-2411-340: Significant work has been done in tasks such as language modeling, word and sentence representation, POS(Parts of speech) tagging, sentiment analysis, textual question answering, etc. \par The problem of VQA is that given any image and any natural language question about that image, a correct natural language answer has to be produced.

elements.sent-2411-341: Despite this simple description, VQA encompasses a lot of sub-problems such as:.
elements.sent-2411-342: Object recognition: What is behind the chair?.
elements.sent-2411-343: Object detection: Are there any people in the image?.
elements.sent-2411-344: Counting: How many dogs are there?.
elements.sent-2411-346: Attribute classification: Is the person happy?.
elements.sent-2411-347: In VQA, the image has to be understood with the context given by the question.
elements.sent-2411-348: The same image has to be “seen” differently for different questions.
elements.sent-2411-349: So proper understanding of the question is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-350: Questions can be simple or complex.
elements.sent-2411-351: Sometimes the reasoning process only involves a single object, but it can also involve multiple objects, their attributes, and their relations.
elements.sent-2411-352: An obvious one is to help blind people understand their surroundings.
elements.sent-2411-353: Searching for images can be made easier as the user only has to ask a question, and then image results are generated based on the query.
elements.sent-2411-354: But the ultimate aim of the VQA problem is to pass the Visual Turing Test, which is the ultimate milestone for any AI research.
elements.sent-2411-355: A significant AI must be able to understand and utilize visual information correctly.
elements.sent-2411-356: VQA is a must to test this understanding.
elements.sent-2411-357: We divide VQA datasets into four categories: 1) General; 2) Synthetic; 3) Diagnostic, and 4) Knowledge-Based.
elements.sent-2411-358: General datasets are large datasets consisting of natural images and human-generated QA pairs.
elements.sent-2411-359: Synthetic datasets contain computer-generated images and questions that are constructed from a fixed number of hand-made templates.
elements.sent-2411-360: Diagnostic datasets are smaller datasets that can be used to isolate and evaluate a specific capability of a model.
elements.sent-2411-361: Finally, knowledge-based datasets provide an external knowledge base that can be queried for extra information.
elements.sent-2411-362: General datasets are the largest, richest, and most used datasets in VQA.
elements.sent-2411-363: General datasets contain many thousands of real-world images from mainstream image datasets like MS-COCO\cite{mscoco and Imagenet\cite{imagenet .}}
elements.sent-2411-364: These datasets are notable for their large scope and diversity.
elements.sent-2411-365: This variety is important as VQA datasets need to reflect the general nature of VQA.
elements.sent-2411-366: Although these datasets do not necessarily capture the endless complexity and variety of visuals in real life, they achieve a close approximation.
elements.sent-2411-367: COCO-QA was one of the first VQA datasets.
elements.sent-2411-368: It consists of 123K images from MS-COCO.
elements.sent-2411-369: QA pairs were automatically generated from image descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-370: The dataset contains very basic questions and only a few question types.
elements.sent-2411-371: Questions are mainly about object presence, number, color, and location.
elements.sent-2411-372: VQA-v1\cite{vqa-v1 (often called the “VQA dataset”) was the first significantly large VQA dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-373: It is divided into two parts, real and abstract.
elements.sent-2411-374: VQA-real consists of real-life images containing multiple objects and a rich, complex mixture of visual information.
elements.sent-2411-375: In contrast, VQA-abstract contains simple artificial scenes generated using clipart software.
elements.sent-2411-376: We talk about vqa-abstract in the synthetic section.
elements.sent-2411-377: Questions and answers were generated using Amazon Mechanical Turk(AMT) workers who were tasked with asking questions that will stump a smart robot.
elements.sent-2411-378: There are severe biases in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-379: For example, a bias of 55.86\% for “yes” in “yes/no” questions and “2” being the most popular answer for “number” questions\cite{vqa-v1 .}
elements.sent-2411-380: Many questions also have a low level of inter-human agreement.
elements.sent-2411-381: Many questions ask for subjective answers and opinions which can not be evaluated for correctness.
elements.sent-2411-382: VQA-v2\cite{vqa-v2 (also known as balanced VQA) tries to balance the biases in the original VQA-v1 dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-383: The authors believed that the original VQA dataset contained significant biases and language priors which enabled models to improve their accuracy by simply leveraging those biases without actually looking at the image.
elements.sent-2411-384: VQA-v2 tries to counter this by having two similar images with two different correct answers for the same question.
elements.sent-2411-385: As a result, the model is forced to look at the image to deduce the correct answer.

elements.sent-2411-386: Among the VQA datasets, visual genome(VG)\cite{visualgenome is one of the richest in terms of information.}
elements.sent-2411-387: It contains over 100K images where each image has an average of 21 objects, 18 attributes, and 18 pairwise relationships between objects.
elements.sent-2411-388: The Visual Genome dataset consists of seven main components: 1) region descriptions; 2) objects; 3) attributes; 4) relationships; 5) region graphs; 6) scene graphs; and 7) QA pairs.
elements.sent-2411-389: It is the first VQA dataset to provide a formalized structural representation of an image through scene graphs.
elements.sent-2411-390: Questions were collected specifically so that they were not ambiguous or speculative but answerable if and only if the image is shown.
elements.sent-2411-391: A notable difference between Visual Genome's question distribution and VQA-v1’s is that the authors focus on ensuring that all categories of questions are adequately represented while in VQA-v1, 32.37\% of the questions are “yes/no” binary questions.
elements.sent-2411-392: Visual genome actually excludes binary questions in order to encourage more difficult QA pairs.

elements.sent-2411-393: Visual7W\cite{visual7w establishes a semantic link between textual descriptions and image regions through object-level grounding.}
elements.sent-2411-394: For each word that indicates an object in the question, the bounding boxes of the corresponding object in the image are provided.
elements.sent-2411-395: This helps the model to better associate objects mentioned in the question with objects present in the image.
elements.sent-2411-396: It also enables the model to provide visual answers.
elements.sent-2411-397: Visual7W provides 7 types of questions: what, where, when, who, why, how, and which.
elements.sent-2411-398: The first 6 question types form the “telling” questions which require descriptive answers while the “which” category forms the “pointing” questions that have to point out an object in the image as the answer.

elements.sent-2411-399: Task Driven Image Understanding Challenge(TDIUC)\cite{tdiuc is specifically tailored to remove biases that plagued previous datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-400: It contains about 1.6 million questions about 160K images.
elements.sent-2411-401: The questions are divided into 12 distinct categories each of which represents a specific vision task that a good VQA model should be able to solve.
elements.sent-2411-402: Previous models had severe biases in their distribution of different question types and in the distribution of answers within each question type.
elements.sent-2411-403: TDIUC tries to mitigate these biases as much as possible.
elements.sent-2411-404: It also proposes new evaluation metrics which better evaluate a model's ability to answer each question type.
elements.sent-2411-405: In previous models, doing well on some classes of questions was rewarded more than others.
elements.sent-2411-406: For example, in VQA-v1, improving accuracy on “Is/Are” questions by 15\% will increase overall accuracy by over 5\% but answering all “Why/Where” questions correctly will increase overall accuracy by only 4.1\%.
elements.sent-2411-407: TDIUC also introduces absurd questions which should be answered with does not apply.
elements.sent-2411-408: It should be noted however that there are only about a thousand unique answers due to most answers being restricted to one or two words.
elements.sent-2411-409: GQA\cite{gqa is a dataset that marries compositional reasoning with real-world images.}
elements.sent-2411-410: It provides a wealth of data consisting of 22M questions for 113k images.
elements.sent-2411-411: Like Visual Genome, it provides rich, structural, formalized image information through scene graphs.
elements.sent-2411-412: But unlike Visual Genome and like CLEVR\cite{clevr , it also provides formal, structural representations of questions in the form of functional programs that detail the reasoning steps needed to answer that question.}
elements.sent-2411-413: GQA is richer than CLEVR in terms of the variety of object classes and properties as it uses real images instead of synthetic ones.
elements.sent-2411-414: Although GQA questions are automatically generated, they are not completely synthetic like CLEVR.
elements.sent-2411-415: Grammatical rules learned from natural language questions and the use of probabilistic grammar makes GQA questions more human-like and more immune to memorization of language priors.
elements.sent-2411-416: Moreover, sampling and balancing are performed to reduce skews and biases in the question and answer distribution.
elements.sent-2411-417: As questions are not generated by humans, many human-produced biases are not present.
elements.sent-2411-418: Finally, GQA provides new evaluation metrics which better facilitate evaluating a model's true capability.

elements.sent-2411-419: Synthetic datasets contain artificial images, produced using software, instead of real images.
elements.sent-2411-420: A good VQA model should be able to perform well on both real and synthetic data like humans do.
elements.sent-2411-421: Synthetic datasets are easier, less expensive, and less time-consuming to produce as the building of a large dataset can be automated.
elements.sent-2411-422: Synthetic datasets can be tailored so that performing well on them requires better reasoning and composition skills.
elements.sent-2411-423: VQA abstract is a subset of the VQA-v1 dataset consisting of 50K artificial scenes.
elements.sent-2411-424: The images were generated using clip-art software.
elements.sent-2411-425: VQA abstract scenes are relatively simple compared to its real counterpart and contain less variety and noise.
elements.sent-2411-426: It was designed this way so a model could focus more on high level reasoning than low level vision tasks.
elements.sent-2411-427: Like VQA-v2 tried to balance VQA-v1, Yin and Yang\cite{yinyang tries to balance the VQA-abstract dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-428: They only focus on the yes/no questions and show that models have been using language biases to gain accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-429: As a solution they balance the number of “yes” and “no” questions.
elements.sent-2411-430: They also provide complimentary scenes so that each question has a “yes” answer for one scene and “no” for the other.
elements.sent-2411-431: SHAPES is a small dataset introduced in \cite{nmn , consisting of 64 images that are composed by arranging colored geometric shapes in different spatial orientations.}
elements.sent-2411-432: Each image has the same 244 “yes/no” questions resulting in 15,616 questions.
elements.sent-2411-433: Due to it's small size, it can only be used for diagnostic purposes to evaluate a model's spatial reasoning skills.
elements.sent-2411-434: CLEVR\cite{clevr is a dataset built solely to evaluate a model's high level reasoning ability.}
elements.sent-2411-435: CLEVR contains 100k synthetic images and 1M artificially generated questions.
elements.sent-2411-436: Scenes are presented as collections of objects annotated with shape, size, color, material, and position on the ground-plane.
elements.sent-2411-437: The CLEVR universe contains three object shapes that come in two absolute sizes, two materials, and eight colors.
elements.sent-2411-438: Objects are spatially related via four relationships: “left”, “right”, “behind” and “in front”.
elements.sent-2411-439: CLEVR questions are automatically generated from hand-made templates.. \par.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=8cm, height=7cm, keepaspectratio]{clevr.png} \caption{An example from CLEVR. Figure from \cite{clevr}}
elements.sent-2411-440: A unique feature of CLEVR is that it provides structural representations of questions as extra annotation.
elements.sent-2411-441: Each question is represented by a sequence of functions which reflect the correct reasoning steps.
elements.sent-2411-442: CLEVR questions are longer and more complex than questions in general VQA datasets.
elements.sent-2411-443: This allows CLEVR to generate a greater variety of unique questions which mitigates question conditional biases.
elements.sent-2411-444: Long questions also correlate to longer reasoning steps necessary to arrive at the answer.
elements.sent-2411-445: Models can overfit due to smaller variety of objects and object features.
elements.sent-2411-446: To test this and to also test compositional generalization, the authors synthesized two new versions of CLEVR: 1) In condition A, all cubes are gray, blue, brown, or yellow and all cylinders are red, green, purple, or cyan; 2) In condition B, these shapes swap color palettes.
elements.sent-2411-447: Both conditions contain spheres of all eight colors.
elements.sent-2411-448: Good models should be able to train on one set and perform well on the other.
elements.sent-2411-449: As CLEVR does not contain real images or natural language questions, the authors suggest using it only as a diagnostic tool to test a model's compositional ability.
elements.sent-2411-450: CLEVR also has a CLEVR-humans variation where the questions are human provided natural language questions.
elements.sent-2411-451: Diagnostic datasets are specialized in the sense that they test a model's ability in a particular area.
elements.sent-2411-452: They are usually small in size and are meant to complement larger, more general datasets by diagnosing the model's performance in a distinct area which may not have pronounced results in the more general dataset.

elements.sent-2411-453: C-VQA\cite{cvqa is a rearranged version of VQA-v1.}
elements.sent-2411-454: It is meant to help evaluate a model's compositional ability, i.e., the ability to answer questions about unseen compositions of seen concepts.
elements.sent-2411-455: For instance, a model is said to be compositional if it can correctly answer [“What color are the horses”, “white”] without seeing this QA pair during training, but perhaps having seen [“What color are the horses?”, “red”] and [“What color are the dogs?”, “white”].
elements.sent-2411-456: C-VQA is created by rearranging the train and val splits of VQA-v1 so that the QA pairs in C-VQA test split are compositionally novel with respect to those in C-VQA train split.
elements.sent-2411-457: For instance, “tennis” is the most frequent answer for the question type “what sport” in C-VQA train split whereas “skiing” is the most frequent answer for the same question type in C-VQA test split.
elements.sent-2411-458: However for VQA-v1, “tennis” is the most frequent answer for both the train and val splits.
elements.sent-2411-459: Like C-VQA, VQA-CP\cite{vqacp reorganizes VQA-v1 and VQA-v2 so that the answer distribution of each question type is different in the train and test splits.}
elements.figure-2411-010: \subfloat[ ]{{\includegraphics[width=4cm, height=3.5cm]{greenplate.png} }} \quad \subfloat[ ]{{\includegraphics[width=4cm, height=3.5cm]{stoplights.png} }} \subcaption*{Training} \subfloat[ ]{{\includegraphics[width=4cm, height=3.5cm]{greenstoplight.png} }} \quad \subfloat[ ]{{\includegraphics[width=4cm, height=3.5cm]{redplate.png} }} \subcaption*{Testing} \caption{An example from CVQA. Figure from \cite{cvqa}}
elements.sent-2411-460: VQA-E\cite{vqa-e and VQA-X\cite{vqa-x argue that a VQA model should provide an explanation along with the answer so that it can be verified that the answer was deduced properly without exploiting dataset biases.}}
elements.sent-2411-461: To facilitate this, both datasets provide explanations along with the usual image-question-answer triplet in its training set.
elements.sent-2411-462: This explanation annotation can teach a model to produce explanations for unseen instances.
elements.sent-2411-463: VQA-E contains 270K textual explanations for 270K QA pairs and 108K images while VQA-X is smaller containing 42K explanations for 33K QA pairs and 28K images.
elements.sent-2411-464: Explanations in VQA-X are human-provided while in VQA-E they are automatically generated.
elements.sent-2411-465: VQA-X also provides visual explanations in the form of human-annotated attention maps for its images.
elements.sent-2411-466: VQA-HAT\cite{vqahat is another dataset that provides attention annotations.}
elements.sent-2411-467: To collect data for VQA-HAT, human subjects were presented with a blurred image and then were asked a question about that image.
elements.sent-2411-468: They were instructed to deblur regions in the image that would help them to answer the question correctly.
elements.sent-2411-469: The regions they chose to deblur are thought to correspond to the regions humans naturally choose to focus on in order to deduce the answer.
elements.sent-2411-470: VQA-Rephrasings\cite{vqa-rephrasings shows that most state-of-the-art VQA models are notoriously brittle to linguistic variations in questions.}
elements.sent-2411-471: VQA-Rephrasings provides 3 human-provided rephrasings for 40k questions spanning 40k images from the VQA-v2 validation dataset.
elements.sent-2411-472: They also provide a model-agnostic method to improve the ability of answering rephrased questions.
elements.figure-2411-011: \subfloat[ ]{{\includegraphics[width=4cm, height=5.5cm]{con_1.png} }} \quad \subfloat[ ]{{\includegraphics[width=4cm, height=5.5cm]{con_2.png} }} \caption{An example from ConVQA. Figure from \cite{convqa}}
elements.sent-2411-473: ConVQA\cite{convqa introduces the task of belief consistency in VQA models, i.e., the task of consistently answering questions that differ in linguistics but share the same semantics.}
elements.sent-2411-474: For example, if the answer to “Is it a vegetarian pizza?” is “yes”, then the answer to “Is there pepperoni on the pizza?” should be “no”.
elements.sent-2411-475: Previous VQA datasets did not have such multiple questions about a single concept to test the consistency of a model.
elements.sent-2411-476: There are a total of 6,792,068 QA pairs in 1,530,979 consistent sets over 108,077 images in ConVQA.
elements.figure-2411-012: \begin{tabular}{ |M{1cm}|M{1cm}|M{1cm}|M{0.5cm}|M{1cm}|M{2cm}|M{1cm}|M{2cm}|M{2cm}|M{2cm}| } Dataset Number of Images Number of QA pairs Type Question types Image source QA source Metric External knowledge annotation Knowledge base used in construction FVQA\cite{fvqa} 2190 5826 OE 32 MS COCO and imagenet Manual Exact matching, WUPS Supporting fact (arg1, rel, arg2) Combination of DBpedia, WebChild, and ConceptNet KB-VQA\cite{kvqa} 700 2402 OE 23 MS COCO Mixed Exact matching, WUPS, Manual none DBpedia OK-VQA\cite{okvqa} 14K 14K OE 10 MS COCO Manual Vqa metric none none \end{tabular} \caption{Statistics of KB datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-477: HowManyQA (introduced in \cite{irlc ) was made by combining counting questions from VQA-v2 and Visual Genome resulting in 106,356 counting questions.}
elements.sent-2411-478: TallyQA\cite{tallyqa points out that most counting questions in VQA datasets and HowManyQA are simple which require nothing more than object detection such as, “How many dogs are there?”.}
elements.sent-2411-479: They present a dataset which contains a large number of complex counting questions, for example, “How many dogs are laying on the grass?”.
elements.sent-2411-480: These questions require more complex reasoning about objects, their attributes and their relationships with other objects and background regions.
elements.sent-2411-481: TallyQA contains 287,907 questions of which 76,477 are complex questions.
elements.figure-2411-013: \subfloat[ ]{{\includegraphics[width=4cm, height=4cm]{stop.png} }} \subfloat[ ]{{\includegraphics[width=4cm, height=4cm]{train.png} }} \quad \caption{An example from ST-VQA. Figure from \cite{stvqa}}
elements.sent-2411-482: A lot of questions humans ask about an image tend to be about text in the image, for example, “What is the number on the license plate?”.
elements.sent-2411-483: Such types of questions are rare in general VQA datasets.
elements.sent-2411-484: TextVQA\cite{textvqa contains 45,336 questions about 28,408 images that require reasoning involving the text present in the image.}
elements.sent-2411-485: Each image-question pair has 10 ground truth answers provided by humans.
elements.sent-2411-486: ST-VQA\cite{stvqa contains 31,791 scene text related questions about 23,038 images.}
elements.sent-2411-487: ST-VQA ensures that all questions are unambiguous and all images contain a minimum amount of text.
elements.sent-2411-488: It ensures bias is not present in question and answer distribution.
elements.sent-2411-489: It also proposes a new evaluation metric more suited for this particular task.
elements.sent-2411-490: Sometimes it is not possible to answer a question with only the information present in the image.
elements.sent-2411-491: In such cases, the required knowledge has to be acquired from external sources.
elements.sent-2411-492: This is where KB datasets come in.
elements.sent-2411-493: They provide questions that require finding and using external knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-494: KB datasets can teach a model to know when it needs to search for absent knowledge and how to acquire that knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-495: Even the largest VQA datasets can not contain all real-world concepts.
elements.sent-2411-496: So VQA models should know how to acquire knowledge about unseen concepts to achieve true generalizability.
elements.sent-2411-497: It should be noted that KB datasets are different from EKBs or External Knowledge Bases which are large structured databases compiling various world information.
elements.sent-2411-498: Examples of such EKBs are, large scale KBs constructed by human annotation, e.g., DBpedia\cite{dbpedia , Freebase\cite{freebase , Wikidata\cite{wikidata and automatic extraction from unstructured/semi-structured data, e.g., YAGO\cite{yago2 ,\cite{yago3 , OpenIE\cite{openie1 ,\cite{openie2 ,\cite{openie3 , NELL\cite{nell , NEIL\cite{neil , WebChild\cite{webchild , ConceptNet\cite{conceptnet .}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-499: FVQA\cite{fvqa is a dataset where most of the questions(5826 questions about 2190 images) require external information to answer.}
elements.sent-2411-500: To support this, FVQA adds an additional “supporting fact” to each image-question-answer triplet.
elements.sent-2411-501: Each supporting-fact is represented as a structural triplet, such as (Cat, CapableOf, ClimbingTrees), which can be used to answer the question.
elements.sent-2411-502: These facts mimic common-sense and external knowledge that help humans(as such should help VQA models too) answer questions that require outside knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-503: By using these facts, FVQA teaches a model to look for the appropriate supporting fact from a facts-database and use that to deduce the answer.
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=10cm, height=6cm, keepaspectratio]{fvqa.PNG} \caption{An example from FVQA. Figure from \cite{fvqa}}
elements.sent-2411-504: KB-VQA\cite{kvqa is a small dataset containing 2402 questions about 700 images based on 23 question templates.}
elements.sent-2411-505: Among these, 1256 are visual questions(does not require external information), 883 require common-sense knowledge and 263 require querying an external knowledge base.
elements.sent-2411-507: All questions were collected so that they require outside knowledge to answer.
elements.sent-2411-508: OK-VQA is larger than both FVQA and KB-VQA.
elements.sent-2411-509: Care was taken so that various biases such as, bias in answer distribution were reduced as much as possible.
elements.sent-2411-510: A model's performance being correctly evaluated depends on the evaluation metric used.
elements.sent-2411-511: Unfortunately, a major problem of VQA is that there is no widely agreed upon evaluation metric.
elements.sent-2411-512: Many different metrics have been proposed.
elements.sent-2411-513: The simplest metric where predictions are considered correct if they match the answer exactly.
elements.sent-2411-514: There are obvious problems with this approach.
elements.sent-2411-515: Many questions have multiple valid answers.
elements.sent-2411-516: The number of valid answers increases with the number of words.
elements.sent-2411-517: This metric can be used for datasets where most of the answers are single-word.
elements.sent-2411-518: But for datasets like Visual Genome where 27\% of answers contain three or more words, this metric performs poorly.
elements.sent-2411-519: WUPS or Wu-Palmer similarity test\cite{wups is a metric where the predicted answer is given a rating between $0$ and $1$ with respect to the ground-truth answer.}
elements.sent-2411-520: The ranking is calculated by tracing their common subsequence in a taxonomy tree and it reflects how “semantically” similar the two words are.
elements.sent-2411-521: For example, “bird” and “eagle” would have a rating closer to $1$ whereas “bird” and “dog” would have a lower rating.
elements.sent-2411-522: But some word pairs are ranked high in this scheme even though they are very loosely related.
elements.sent-2411-523: Moreover, some questions are such that only one answer is correct whereas other answers, despite being semantically similar, are wrong.
elements.sent-2411-524: For example, a question with “What color ..” can only have one correct answer, e.g., “red” while other colors like “black” are wrong answers.
elements.sent-2411-525: This was the metric used for the VQA-v1 dataset.
elements.sent-2411-526: The metric is, \begin{equation}Accuracy_{VQA} = min(\frac{n}{3},1)\end{equation} where $n$ is the number of annotators with the same answer as the predicted one.
elements.sent-2411-527: Basically, this metric considers any prediction correct if it matches with the answers of at least 3 annotators.
elements.sent-2411-528: This scheme allows multiple valid answers.
elements.sent-2411-529: There are some problems with this metric.
elements.sent-2411-530: It turns out that not all questions have annotators agreeing what the answer should be.
elements.sent-2411-531: Especially 59\% of “why” questions have no answer with more than two annotators which means the metric will consider any answer for these questions incorrect.
elements.sent-2411-532: Moreover, there are questions where directly conflicting answers, like “yes” and “no”, have at least three annotators.
elements.sent-2411-533: This means that both “yes” and “no” are considered valid answers for these questions.
elements.sent-2411-534: Because of skewed question and answer distribution, normal evaluation metrics do not capture how well a model is performing across all question and answer categories.
elements.sent-2411-535: TDIUC\cite{tdiuc introduces A-MPT(Arithmetic mean per type) and H-MPT(Harmonic mean per type).}
elements.sent-2411-536: A-MPT evaluates average accuracy like previous metrics.
elements.sent-2411-537: H-MPT measures the ability of a system to have high scores across all question-types and is skewed towards the lowest performing categories.
elements.sent-2411-538: TDIUC also introduces N-MPT(normalized MPT) to compensate for bias in the distribution of answers within each question-type.
elements.sent-2411-539: This is done by computing the accuracy for each unique answer separately within a question-type and then averaging them together.
elements.sent-2411-540: A large discrepancy between unnormalized and normalized scores suggests that the model is not generalizing to rarer answers.
elements.sent-2411-541: GQA\cite{gqa introduces 5 new metrics for detailed analysis of the true capability of a model.}
elements.sent-2411-542: Consistency checks that the model provides consistent answers for different questions based on the same semantics.
elements.sent-2411-543: For example, If the model answers the question “What is the color of the apple to the right of the plate?” correctly with red, then it should also be able to infer the answer to the question “Is the plate to the left of the red apple?”.
elements.sent-2411-544: Validity checks whether a given answer is in the question scope, e.g., responding some color, and not some size, to a color question.
elements.sent-2411-545: Plausibility score goes a step further measuring whether the answer makes sense given the question.
elements.sent-2411-546: For example, red and green are plausible apple colors and conversely purple is implausible.
elements.sent-2411-547: Distribution measures the overall match between the true answer distribution and the predicted distribution.
elements.sent-2411-548: It allows us to see if the model has generalized to rarer answers.
elements.sent-2411-549: For attention-based models, Grounding checks whether the model attends to regions within the image that are relevant to the question.
elements.sent-2411-550: This metric evaluates the degree to which the model grounds its reasoning in the image rather than just making educated guesses.
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=18cm, height=8cm, keepaspectratio]{diagram.png} \caption{Basic approach of VQA algorithms}
elements.sent-2411-551: Most VQA algorithms follow a basic structure:.
elements.sent-2411-553: Image representation in VQA is done using either pre-trained CNNs(Convolutional Neural Network) or object detectors.
elements.sent-2411-554: Some examples of pre-trained CNNs that have been used are GoogLeNet\cite{googlenet , VGGNet\cite{vggnet , ResNet\cite{resnet .}}}
elements.sent-2411-555: Among these, ResNet seems to consistently give higher accuracies than other CNNs.
elements.sent-2411-556: For detecting object-regions, the primary choice has been Faster R-CNN\cite{fasterr-cnn .}
elements.sent-2411-557: Mask R-CNN\cite{maskr-cnn is a more recent detector that seems to be more robust and better at detection than Faster R-CNN.}
elements.sent-2411-558: Images from these datasets were also used to construct most general VQA datasets which makes transfer learning possible.
elements.sent-2411-559: When given an input image, a CNN goes through several convolution and pooling layers to produce a $C\times W\times H$ shaped output.
elements.sent-2411-560: Here, $W$ and $H$ refers to the modified width and height of the image and $C$ refers to the number of convolution filters.
elements.sent-2411-561: Each convolution filter can be thought to detect a certain pattern.
elements.sent-2411-563: Each bounding box usually contains an object belonging to a specific object class.
elements.sent-2411-564: Here “object” can mean things like sky, cloud, water which aren't considered objects in the traditional sense.
elements.sent-2411-565: Faster R-CNN produces rectangular bounding boxes which means an object doesn't always fit inside its box.
elements.sent-2411-566: Object detection also suffers from overlapping issues.
elements.sent-2411-567: The same object can be part of multiple bounding boxes all overlapping each other.
elements.sent-2411-568: For example, Consider an image containing a person wearing a shirt.
elements.sent-2411-569: The shirt can be part of two overlapping bounding boxes, the bounding box containing 'shirt' and the bounding box containing 'person'.
elements.sent-2411-570: Bounding boxes of different objects can also overlap each other especially if one object occludes another object behind it.
elements.sent-2411-571: Mask R-CNN\cite{maskr-cnn produces polygonal masks which reduces these problems.}
elements.sent-2411-572: Most VQA algorithms using object detectors typically use the top 24-32 predicted objects from the image.\par.
elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=15cm, height=6cm, keepaspectratio]{img-rep1.PNG} \caption{CNN(left) and Faster R-CNN(right). Figure from \cite{butd}}
elements.sent-2411-573: According to the method used, image representation can be divided into grid-based and object-based approaches.
elements.sent-2411-574: CNNs divide any image into a uniform grid(typically $14\times 14$) and object detectors divide the image into multiple object-regions(typically 32).
elements.sent-2411-575: A uniform grid structure means sometimes an object can overlap into several adjacent cells.
elements.sent-2411-576: Irrelevant noise can be an issue as the whole image is used but this can be mitigated through attention mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-577: Object detectors break the image down into salient objects which may provide a stronger training signal as the scene is organised into meaningful units.
elements.sent-2411-578: But they ignore all area outside of the predicted bounding boxes which may result in information loss.
elements.sent-2411-579: Visual information about broader semantics such as scene and sentiment is also lost.
elements.sent-2411-580: Earlier models universally used pre-trained CNN features.
elements.sent-2411-581: BUTD\cite{butd was the first major VQA model to utilize object detection.}
elements.sent-2411-582: Since BUTD, most models have opted to use Faster R-CNN pre-trained on the Visual Genome object detection task.
elements.sent-2411-583: But recently \cite{grid-defense have presented findings arguing for using grid features.}
elements.sent-2411-584: Like BUTD, \cite{grid-defense also pre-trains Faster R-CNN for VG object detection but unlike BUTD, they discard region prediction during the inference step and directly use the grid features from the last convolution layer.}
elements.sent-2411-585: They identify that pre-training for object detection is an important step that incorporates richer visual understanding but extracting object features are not essential for answering visual questions.. \par.
elements.sent-2411-586: Question representation in VQA is usually done by first embedding individual words and then using an RNN or a CNN to produce an embedding of the entire question.
elements.sent-2411-587: Then they either used them directly or fed them to an RNN or CNN.
elements.sent-2411-588: Later models used a pre-trained word embedding model such as: Word2Vec\cite{word2vec or GloVe\cite{glove .}}
elements.sent-2411-589: Word2Vec was built using skip-gram and negative sampling.
elements.sent-2411-590: It produces similar vectors for words with similar semantic meanings.
elements.sent-2411-591: GloVe uses word-word co-occurrence probability on the basis that words that occur frequently together have a higher probability of being semantically connected.
elements.sent-2411-592: More recent works have utilized language models such as, ELMo\cite{elmo , BERT\cite{bert , and GPT\cite{gpt-1}\cite{gpt-2}\cite{gpt-3 which have set new state-of-the-art performances across a range of NLP tasks.}}}
elements.sent-2411-593: These models are pre-trained on a huge amount of natural language data and can be expected to produce sufficiently useful representations for the VQA task.
elements.sent-2411-594: Another option is to also use a CNN for question representation.
elements.sent-2411-595: Some models skip the word embedding and directly embed the whole question using a sentence embedding model like Skip-thought\cite{skip-thought .}
elements.sent-2411-596: Another question to ask is “Does RNN work better than CNN?”.
elements.sent-2411-597: Although RNNs usually outperform CNN in many NLP related tasks, the same can not be definitely said for VQA.
elements.sent-2411-598: They conclude that CNN is better suited for VQA than RNN because of the limited length of question sentences which prevents RNNs from using their full potential.
elements.sent-2411-599: Understanding a question properly requires identifying keywords which CNN does better as it treats individual words as features.
elements.sent-2411-600: We think that comparison between CNN and RNN for VQA is something that should be looked into further.
elements.sent-2411-601: For the network to predict answers, it has to jointly reason over both question and image.
elements.sent-2411-602: The interaction of the visual and textual domain in VQA is either done directly through multimodal fusion or indirectly through attention mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-603: We will talk about both methods in the upcoming sections.
elements.sent-2411-604: For open-ended VQA, answers are predicted either through a generative approach or a non-generative approach.
elements.sent-2411-605: In the non-generative approach, all or the most frequent unique answers in the dataset are set as individual classes.
elements.sent-2411-606: This approach is easier to evaluate and implement but has the obvious shortcoming of not being able to predict answers not seen during training.
elements.sent-2411-607: Another option is to generate the answer word by word using an RNN but there are no good methods of evaluating the generated answers which means almost all models adopt the non-generative approach.
elements.sent-2411-608: For multiple-choice VQA, answer prediction is treated as a ranking problem where each question, image and answer trio gets a score and the answer with the highest score is selected.
elements.sent-2411-609: In single-label classification, models output a probability distribution over the answer classes using softmax.
elements.sent-2411-610: In this approach, models are trained to maximise probability for a single correct answer, usually the ground truth answer most annotators agree on.
elements.sent-2411-611: It can be argued that this single-label approach results in a poorer training signal and a multi-label regressive approach fares better.
elements.sent-2411-612: Most VQA questions have multiple correct answers, so it makes sense to produce multiple labels.
elements.sent-2411-613: The most popular VQA dataset VQA-v1 actually provides multiple ground truth answers.
elements.sent-2411-614: The answers are assigned soft accuracies according to the metric, $min(\frac{\text{\# humans that provided that answer {3 ,1)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-615: This makes evaluating multiple correct answers possible as answers with fewer annotators still get a partial score.
elements.sent-2411-616: BUTD\cite{butd was the first model to use the answer scores from VQA-v1 as soft targets and cast the answering task as a regression of scores for candidate answers instead of traditional classification.}
elements.sent-2411-617: BUTD argues that this method is much more effective than simple softmax classification because it allows for producing multiple answers and models the distribution of human provided annotations better.
elements.sent-2411-618: Following BUTD, most recent models use the multi-label regression approach.
elements.sent-2411-619: But this handicaps semantic understanding as there is now a gap between how the model interprets the question and how it interprets the answer, despite both coming from the same semantic space.
elements.sent-2411-620: For example, for a question with the correct answer of “dog”, most models will penalize “cat” and “german shephard” equally even though “german shephard” comes closer to the correct answer.
elements.sent-2411-621: Some works have tried to close this gap by projecting the answers into the same semantic space as the questions.
elements.sent-2411-622: Just like the questions, the answers are turned into vectors and the task of answering becomes regressing the answer vector.
elements.sent-2411-623: This way the model can have a more accurate view of the answer space where “german shephard” is closer to “dog” than “cat” resulting in a better training signal and a more nuanced understanding.
elements.sent-2411-624: In order to perform joint reasoning on a QA pair, information from the two modalities have to mix and interact.
elements.sent-2411-625: This can be achieved by multimodal fusion.
elements.sent-2411-626: We divide fusion in VQA into two types, vector operation and bilinear pooling.
elements.sent-2411-627: Early VQA models used vector addition, multiplication and concatenation to perform fusion.
elements.sent-2411-628: They are relatively simple in terms of complexity and computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-629: In vector addition and multiplication, question and image features are projected linearly through fully-connected layers to match their dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-630: Although vector operations are simple to implement, they do not result in good accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-631: Among the three vector operations, concatenation usually has the worst performance while multiplication seems to fare the best.
elements.sent-2411-632: Bilinear pooling\cite{bilinear computes the outer product of the question and image feature vectors.}
elements.sent-2411-633: The outer product of two vectors gives a complete representation of all possible interactions between the elements of the vectors.
elements.sent-2411-634: If $z_i$ is the $i$-th output(with bias terms omitted) then, \begin{equation}z_i = x^TW_iy\end{equation} where $x \epsilon \mathbb{R ^m $, $y \epsilon \mathbb{R ^n $, $z \epsilon \mathbb{R ^o $ and $W \epsilon \mathbb{R ^{m \times n \times o $.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-635: But in VQA, directly computing the outer product is infeasible.
elements.sent-2411-636: If both image and question feature vectors have dimensions of 2048 and there are 3000 answer classes, we would need to compute 12.5 billion parameters \cite{mcb .}
elements.sent-2411-637: This is computationally expensive and may lead to overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-638: The models we discuss try to control the number of parameters by imposing various restrictions.
elements.sent-2411-639: Ultimately, we will see that these models are just different approaches towards the trade-off between expressibility and complexity or trainability.
elements.sent-2411-640: MCB\cite{mcb utilizes the above approach but replaces the convolution with a more efficient element-wise product in FFT(Fast Fourier Transform) space.}
elements.sent-2411-641: In this way, MCB indirectly computes the outer product.
elements.sent-2411-642: MCB still needs to maintain a large number of parameters to overcome the bias that results from $h$ and $s$ being fixed.
elements.sent-2411-643: MLB tries to mitigate this by decomposing $W$ into $W = UV^T$ which results in, \begin{equation}z_i = 1^T(U_i^Tx \circ V_i^Ty)\end{equation} where $\circ$ is the hadamard product.
elements.sent-2411-645: This decomposition reduces the number of parameters by imposing a restriction on the rank of $W_i$ to be at most $k$ where $k \leq min(m,n)$.
elements.sent-2411-646: To further reduce the order of weight tensors by one, MLB replaces the unit tensor with another matrix $P_i \epsilon \mathbb{R ^{k \times c $ to get, \begin{equation}z_i = P_i^T(U_i^Tx \circ V_i^Ty)\end{equation} $k$ and $c$ are hyperparameters that decide the dimension of the joint embeddings and the dimension of the model output respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-648: It takes many iterations to converge and it is sensitive to hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-649: The authors show that MLB is a special case of MFB with $k = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-650: They also introduce MFH which can perform higher dimensional pooling by stacking multiple MFBs.
elements.sent-2411-651: Matrices $W_q$ and $W_v$ project the question and the image vector into dimensions $t_q$ and $t_v$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-652: These dimensions directly impact the complexity of modeling for each modality.
elements.sent-2411-653: Tensor $\tau_c$ controls the complexity of the interactions between projections produced by $W_q$ and $W_v$.
elements.sent-2411-654: The number of parameters can be controlled by imposing constraints on the rank of $\tau_c$.
elements.sent-2411-655: Finally, the matrix $W_o $ scores each pair of embedding for each class in the answer set.
elements.sent-2411-656: It can be shown that MCB and MLB are special cases of MUTAN.
elements.sent-2411-657: BLOCK aims to achieve a balance between MLB and MUTAN.
elements.sent-2411-658: MLB can be thought of as having the same number of blocks as the rank, each block being of size $(1, 1, 1)$.
elements.sent-2411-659: This means that projections of both modalities can be very high dimensional but interactions between them will be poor.
elements.sent-2411-660: In comparison, MUTAN uses only one block of size $(L, M, N)$ which means better interactions between projections but the projections are modeled less accurately.
elements.sent-2411-661: BLOCK takes a middle approach between these methods and performs better.

elements.sent-2411-662: Attention is the mechanism most widely used by VQA models to improve accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-663: When we are shown an image and asked a question about that image, we usually focus on the particular region of the image that helps us answer the question.
elements.sent-2411-664: VQA models try to replicate this through attention.
elements.sent-2411-665: This improves accuracy because a lot of irrelevant noise is filtered out and the model can focus better on the parts of the image that are actually relevant for answering the question.
elements.sent-2411-666: There are many different types of attention which we discuss below.
elements.sent-2411-667: The first aspect of note is soft and hard attention.
elements.sent-2411-668: Most models use the question to produce an attention map over the image.
elements.sent-2411-669: This map assigns different weights to different regions in the image according to their perceived relevance in answering the question.
elements.sent-2411-670: In soft attention, every region is assigned weights with more weight given to more relevant areas.
elements.sent-2411-671: In hard attention, only regions which are deemed sufficiently relevant are selected for further consideration while all other regions are completely ignored.
elements.sent-2411-672: So, to compare, hard attention will completely ignore some areas of the image while soft attention will pay less attention to those areas.
elements.sent-2411-673: The selected regions in hard attention may go through a further soft attention stage.

elements.sent-2411-674: Attention can also be divided into grid and object based approaches.
elements.sent-2411-675: In the grid based approach, the whole image is divided into a uniform grid where each cell is the same size.
elements.sent-2411-676: Almost all grid based approaches use soft attention.
elements.sent-2411-677: As the image is divided into regions of same size, many regions contain multiple objects.
elements.sent-2411-678: Using hard attention in this case may erase information about multiple objects which could result in significant information loss.
elements.sent-2411-679: In object based approaches, object detectors are used to divide the image into multiple objects.
elements.sent-2411-680: This falls under hard attention as image area that is not contained by the bounding box of any object is completely ignored.
elements.sent-2411-681: This is why some object based approaches also provide the whole image to the model to ensure access to all information.
elements.sent-2411-682: They argue that some questions benefit more from object level attention and some benefit more from a grid based approach.
elements.sent-2411-683: These models extract image features from both grid-based CNN and object-based Faster R-CNN.
elements.sent-2411-684: The question is then used to assign weights to the features.
elements.sent-2411-685: In this way, the question itself determines which image feature should be used to answer the question.
elements.sent-2411-686: Another important concept is bottom-up and top-down attention.
elements.sent-2411-687: This is related to how human brains process vision.
elements.sent-2411-688: In the human visual system, attention can be focused volitionally by top-down signals determined by the current task, e.g., looking for something, and automatically by bottom-up signals associated with unexpected, novel, or salient stimuli\cite{humanbutd1}\cite{humanbutd2 .}
elements.sent-2411-689: Similarly in the context of VQA, a top-down approach means considering the question first and then looking at the image while a bottom-up approach implies looking at the image first and then considering the question.
elements.sent-2411-690: BUTD\cite{butd argues that both types of attention are useful.}
elements.sent-2411-691: BUTD first applies bottom-up attention using Faster R-CNN to detect the most salient objects.
elements.sent-2411-692: This is equivalent to just looking at an image and noting all the prominent objects.
elements.sent-2411-693: BUTD then weighs these objects according to the question.
elements.sent-2411-694: This is top-down attention as visual information is being processed after considering the question.
elements.figure-2411-022: \includegraphics[width=12cm, height=3cm, keepaspectratio]{attention-3.PNG} \caption{Multi-step attention through multiple attention layers in SAN. Figure from \cite{san}}
elements.sent-2411-695: Attention can be single or multi-step.
elements.sent-2411-696: This is also referred to as “glimpse”.
elements.sent-2411-697: A model using multiple glimpses means multiple attention layers have been cascaded one after another.
elements.sent-2411-698: Some questions are simple and only require a single attention step to answer.
elements.sent-2411-699: For example, “What color is the dog?” only requires a single attention step to detect the dog.
elements.sent-2411-700: But many questions require multiple reasoning steps which may also require applying attention multiple times.
elements.sent-2411-701: For example, “What color is the dog standing beside the chair behind the counter?”.
elements.sent-2411-702: This question requires three attention steps.
elements.sent-2411-703: The first one to identify the counter, the second to identify the chair behind the counter, and the third to identify the dog beside the chair.
elements.sent-2411-704: So we see that multi-step attention can lead to improved reasoning skills.

elements.sent-2411-705: Until now we have only discussed image attention but what about attending the question?.
elements.sent-2411-706: HieCoAtt\cite{hiecoatt was the first model to seriously explore question attention.}
elements.sent-2411-707: They argued that just as image attention can filter out noise from the image, question attention can also reduce unwanted features.
elements.sent-2411-708: Just as the question can guide attention on the image, the image can also guide attention on the question.
elements.sent-2411-709: This is referred to as co-attention.
elements.sent-2411-710: Co-attention can take the image into context and apply weights to question words according to how relevant they are for answering that particular image.
elements.sent-2411-711: In co-attention, image and question attend each-other but it is also possible for each modality to apply attention to itself.
elements.sent-2411-713: Self-attention can be applied to the question because not all words in a question are of equal importance.
elements.sent-2411-714: For example, consider the question “What color is the dog?”.
elements.sent-2411-715: Here the question can be shortened to “What color dog” without losing any significant meaning.
elements.sent-2411-716: Self-attention can also be applied to the image.
elements.sent-2411-717: Self-attention for image determines which region or object is important after taking into consideration all other regions and objects.
elements.sent-2411-718: For example, consider an image of a sheep-dog guarding a herd of sheep.
elements.sent-2411-719: Here the individual sheep are less important and the herd is more important as a whole.
elements.sent-2411-720: So self-attention can modify the weights such that the combined weight of all the sheep is equal to the weight of the dog which basically embodies the main relation in the image which is “A dog guarding a herd of sheep”.. \par.
