elements.sent-2411-721: Sometimes the information required to answer a question can not be found in the given image.
elements.sent-2411-722: For example, The question “What do the two animals in the image have in common?” for an image containing a Giraffe and a Lion can not be answered from the image alone.
elements.sent-2411-723: To correctly answer this question, i.e., they are both mammals, the model has to have an idea about the outside concept of “mammal”.
elements.sent-2411-724: As conventional VQA models have no way of gathering knowledge about unseen concepts, they will guess blindly from the answer space of known concepts.
elements.sent-2411-725: Most VQA models have limited knowledge of real-world concepts because the datasets they are trained on are restricted in their size and cannot reflect every combination of every concept.
elements.sent-2411-726: A simple way to solve this problem is to give the model the capability to query an External Knowledge Base or EKB.
elements.sent-2411-727: Construction, organization, and querying of these knowledge bases are problems that have been studied for years.
elements.sent-2411-728: This has led to the development of large-scale KBs constructed by human annotation, e.g., DBpedia\cite{dbpedia , Freebase\cite{freebase , Wikidata\cite{wikidata ) and KBs constructed by automatic extraction from unstructured or semi-structured data, e.g., YAGO\cite{yago2}\cite{yago3 , OpenIE\cite{openie1}\cite{openie2}\cite{openie3 , NELL\cite{nell , NEIL\cite{neil , WebChild\cite{webchild , ConceptNet\cite{conceptnet .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-729: In structured KBs, knowledge is typically represented by a large number of triples of the form (arg1, rel, arg2).
elements.sent-2411-730: A collection of such triples forms a large interlinked graph.
elements.sent-2411-731: Information can be retrieved by using an SQL-like query language.
elements.sent-2411-732: Queries usually return one or multiple sub-graphs satisfying the specified conditions.. \par.
elements.figure-2411-026: \includegraphics[width=8cm, height=8cm, keepaspectratio]{ekb-1.PNG} \caption{Example of an image-question pair that needs external knowledge to answer. Figure adapted from \cite{ama}}
elements.figure-2411-027: \includegraphics[width=10cm, height=6cm, keepaspectratio]{ekb-2.PNG} \caption{An example structure of an EKB. Figure from \cite{fvqa}}
elements.sent-2411-733: EKB models usually try to map image concepts and question concepts to their equivalent KB concepts as extra information about those concepts is likely to be helpful in the answering process.
elements.sent-2411-734: Some models\cite{ahab ,\cite{fvqa do this by building an image-specific knowledge graph.}}
elements.sent-2411-735: Visual concepts such as objects, scenes, attributes, actions, etc., are detected in the image and used to build a scene graph.
elements.sent-2411-736: A knowledge sub-graph is produced by querying the knowledge base with the detected image concepts.
elements.sent-2411-737: The two graphs are combined by linking the visual concepts with their corresponding KB concepts.
elements.sent-2411-738: This combined graph is the image-specific knowledge graph that integrates additional information about various image content.
elements.sent-2411-739: Along with visual concepts, KDMN\cite{kdmn also uses keywords from the question to query the knowledge base.}
elements.sent-2411-740: Thus, its knowledge graph contains question concepts along with visual concepts.
elements.sent-2411-741: ACK\cite{ack uses the image to predict top-5 attributes and uses them to query DBpedia which returns five knowledge paragraphs.}
elements.sent-2411-742: STTF\cite{sttf and OOTB\cite{ootb extract visual concepts from the image and use the question to filter relevant facts from a facts database.}}
elements.sent-2411-743: STTF then selects a top fact by a ranking mechanism and uses this to answer the question whereas OOTB\cite{ootb constructs a graph and uses graph convolution to produce the final answer.}
elements.sent-2411-744: Some of these issues stem from the difficulty of interacting with most KBs.
elements.sent-2411-745: KBs cannot be queried with natural language questions and need specific query formats.
elements.sent-2411-746: There are only a limited number of ways in which a KB can be queried and any question we want to ask the KB must be reducible to one of the available query templates.
elements.sent-2411-747: Parsing the image and the question to produce appropriate queries is still an error-prone process that many EKB models struggle with.
elements.sent-2411-748: Another issue is with integrating knowledge graphs.
elements.sent-2411-749: They are symbolic in nature which makes it difficult to train an EKB model in an end-to-end differentiable manner.
elements.sent-2411-750: Some models work around this by converting the knowledge graph into a continuous vector representation.
elements.sent-2411-751: For example, KDMN\cite{kdmn treats each knowledge triple as a three-word SVO(subject-verb-object) phrase.}
elements.sent-2411-752: It embeds each triple into a common feature space by feeding its word embeddings through an RNN architecture.
elements.sent-2411-753: In this case, the external knowledge is embedded in the same semantic space as the question and the answer.
elements.sent-2411-754: Thus, the model can be trained in an end-to-end manner.
elements.sent-2411-755: Most VQA models do not possess significant reasoning skills.
elements.sent-2411-756: They show good performance on general VQA datasets which lack complex questions.
elements.sent-2411-757: They fail on datasets like CLEVR which was designed specifically to test a model's high level reasoning skill.
elements.sent-2411-758: To correctly reason about complex questions, a model needs to be compositional in nature.
elements.sent-2411-759: By composition, we refer to the ability to break a question down into individual reasoning steps which when done sequentially produces the correct answer.
elements.sent-2411-760: An example of such a question would be “How many large cubes are behind the purple cylinder?”.
elements.sent-2411-761: Answering this question requires detecting the purple cylinder, then filtering the objects behind it, then filtering again to find objects that are both large and cube-shaped, and finally counting them.
elements.sent-2411-762: In contrast, most VQA models are trained to answer simple questions which usually require a single visual verification step.
elements.sent-2411-763: We divide compositional models into two camps, 1) PGE(Program generator and executor) models and 2) Blackbox models.
elements.sent-2411-764: PGE models consist of two parts, program generation and program execution.
elements.sent-2411-765: In the first part, the input question is parsed to produce a program.
elements.sent-2411-766: This program is executed in the second part to obtain the answer.
elements.sent-2411-767: Most complex questions can be mapped to a dependency tree where each node represents a single reasoning step.
elements.sent-2411-768: The correct answer can be found by traversing the tree and processing each node appropriately.
elements.sent-2411-769: After a question has been mapped to a valid tree, the next step is to transform this tree into an executable program by using neural modules.
elements.sent-2411-770: PGE models have a toolbox of neural modules where each module can perform a specific reasoning task such as a “find” module that can locate an object/concept in the image.
elements.sent-2411-771: A neural module is plugged into each node of the tree according to the task that node represents.
elements.sent-2411-772: It is important to note here that these modules are not hard-coded but their parameters are trained just like a neural network.
elements.sent-2411-773: In the next step, the complete network is dynamically assembled by laying out the modules in the tree.
elements.sent-2411-774: The answer is predicted by feeding the inputs to this network.
elements.sent-2411-775: Due to their modular nature, PGE models offer greater transparency and interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-776: As the inference process follows a chain of discrete steps, reasoning failures can be pinpointed to a particular node which is not possible in a traditional end-to-end neural network.
elements.sent-2411-777: This can also be leveraged to produce explanations in order to validate sound reasoning.

elements.sent-2411-778: Earlier models such as NMN\cite{nmn and D-NMN\cite{d-nmn used the Stanford dependency parser to produce the dependency tree.}}
elements.sent-2411-779: But due to the parser being error-prone, the selected layout was often wrong.
elements.sent-2411-780: Later models\cite{n2nmn}\cite{stack-nmn}\cite{xnm treated layout mapping as a sequence-to-sequence learning problem and replaced off-the-shelf parsers with encoder-decoder models.}
elements.sent-2411-781: At first, models like N2NMN\cite{n2nmn selected layouts discreetly which was a non-differentiable process.}
elements.sent-2411-782: This meant that the program generation part of the model had to be optimized using reinforcement learning while the execution part was trained using gradients.
elements.sent-2411-783: This multi-stage joint training was difficult as the program generator needed to produce the right program without understanding what the individual modules meant, and the execution engine had to produce the right answer from a program that might not accurately represent the question.
elements.sent-2411-784: Later models like Stack-NMN\cite{stack-nmn and XNM\cite{xnm converted layout selection into a soft continuous process so that the model could be optimized in a fully differentiable manner using gradient descent.}}
elements.sent-2411-785: DDRprog\cite{ddrprog solved the problem of differentiability by interleaving module prediction and generation, thereby producing the layout “on the fly”.}
elements.sent-2411-786: PTGRN\cite{ptgrn solves it by eliminating the need for module prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-787: They do this by using generic PTGRN modules instead of specialized neural modules.
elements.sent-2411-788: They directly use the dependency tree produced by the Stanford parser as the program layout by replacing each node with a PTGRN node module and each edge with a PTGRN edge module.
elements.sent-2411-789: More recent models like XNM do not require expert layouts and have shown competitive performance on general VQA datasets on par with models that do not use compositional reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-790: This is significant considering that non-compositional models have been shown to utilize various dataset biases while PGE models are less prone to bias due to their modular architecture.
elements.figure-2411-030: \begin{tabular}{ |M{3cm}|M{3cm}| } Model CLEVR FiLM\cite{film} 97.7 CMM\cite{cmm} 98.6 MAC\cite{mac} 98.9 OCCAM\cite{occam} 99.4 \end{tabular} \caption{Accuracy of different Blackbox models.}
elements.sent-2411-791: A major setback for many PGE models is that they rely on program annotations, brittle handcrafted parsers, or expert demonstrations and require relatively complex multi-stage reinforcement learning training schemes.
elements.sent-2411-792: These models are also limited to using a few handcrafted neural modules each with a highly rigid structure.
elements.sent-2411-793: This makes many PGE models inflexible compared to conventional neural network-based VQA models.
elements.sent-2411-794: Blackbox models try to achieve a balance between both worlds.
elements.sent-2411-795: Like PGE models, blackbox models utilize structural priors to a certain extent while retaining the fluidity and dynamic nature of neural network-based models.
elements.sent-2411-796: Blackbox models achieve this by using a single highly flexible blackbox module instead of multiple operation-specific neural modules.
elements.sent-2411-797: Multiple blackbox cells are cascaded one after another to form a long chain.
elements.sent-2411-798: Because of self-attention and residual connections between the cells, this linear network is capable of representing arbitrarily complex acyclic reasoning graphs in a soft manner.
elements.sent-2411-799: Because of the use of a single reusable module and the physically sequential nature of the network, blackbox models manage to circumvent two major bottlenecks of PGE models, namely, module selection and layout selection.
elements.sent-2411-800: This allows blackbox models to be trained in an end-to-end manner using simple backpropagation just like conventional VQA models instead of the complex multi-stage training of PGE models.
elements.sent-2411-801: Though multiple instances of the same cell are used in multiple layers, they only share the general structure.
elements.sent-2411-802: Through training, different cells in different layers learn to perform different reasoning operations.
elements.sent-2411-803: This allows blackbox models to perform long and complex chains of reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-804: Blackbox models retain fluidity and flexibility while maintaining compositional reasoning ability albeit at the cost of some transparency and interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-805: MAC follows a controller-memory scheme where strict separation is maintained between the representation space of the question and the image.
elements.sent-2411-806: This is in contrast to the conventional approach of fusing the question and the image representation in the same semantic space.
elements.sent-2411-807: Each MAC cell consists of three units: a) Control; b) Read; and c) Write.
elements.sent-2411-808: They operate on a control state and a memory state.
elements.sent-2411-809: The control states are determined by the question and the control states in different cells are expected to encode a series of reasoning operations.
elements.sent-2411-810: The read unit extracts the required visual information from the image guided by the control and memory state.
elements.sent-2411-811: After the control unit performs its reasoning operation, the write unit integrates the new information into the memory state.
elements.sent-2411-812: This architecture allows MAC to divide its operation into discrete reasoning steps where the question and the image interact in a controlled way.
elements.sent-2411-813: This makes it easier to interpret the inner workings of a particular MAC cell resulting in better transparency.
elements.sent-2411-814: Similar to MAC, FiLM\cite{film uses the question to guide its computation over the image.}
elements.sent-2411-815: In contrast to MAC, where the question does not influence the image feature extraction process, FiLM uses the question to guide how its visual pipeline extracts features from the image.
elements.sent-2411-816: FiLM consists of several convolution layers like a typical CNN.
elements.sent-2411-817: But in FiLM, each convolution layer is paired with a FiLM layer which controls the feature map computation in that layer.
elements.sent-2411-818: The parameters of each FiLM layer are determined by the question.
elements.sent-2411-819: The intuition is that each FiLM layer will encode the reasoning operation necessary at that stage of the pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-820: CMM\cite{cmm modifies FiLM so that both modalities are influenced by each other.}
elements.sent-2411-821: It argues that, since information comes from multiple modalities, it is not intuitive to assume that one modality(language) is the “program generator”, and the other(image) is the “executor”.
elements.sent-2411-822: One way to avoid making this assumption is to perform multiple steps of reasoning with each modality being generator and executor alternatively in each step.
elements.sent-2411-823: A recent trend in VQA models has been towards explainability.
elements.sent-2411-824: Many have argued that VQA models should be able to explain their answers, i.e., the precise reasoning steps the model followed to deduce the answer.
elements.sent-2411-825: Traditional neural networks act like blackboxes whose inner workings remain opaque to outside observers.
elements.sent-2411-826: This makes it difficult to verify if the network is reasoning correctly.
elements.sent-2411-827: The model may be using dataset biases or merely guessing the answer.
elements.sent-2411-828: There is no way to be sure that the model is producing the right answers for the right reasons.
elements.sent-2411-829: A good AI model should be able to explain its actions and expose the reasoning process it followed.
elements.sent-2411-830: Forcing models to generate explanations ensures trust in VQA systems and exposes models which merely use biases and shortcuts.
elements.sent-2411-831: Explanations force models to reveal their internal process and it can be verified that the correct answer is being predicted logically.
elements.sent-2411-832: In case of the model predicting a wrong answer, the precise step where failure occurred can be pinpointed.
elements.sent-2411-833: Maybe the model failed to understand image content correctly or maybe the model had trouble understanding the question.
elements.sent-2411-835: It could also be that the model understood the image and the question properly but failed to follow the correct reasoning steps.
elements.sent-2411-837: Explanations help to gain a better understanding of how the model is actually operating.
elements.sent-2411-838: So if the model predicts the correct answer, we can make sure it was because it followed proper logical reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-839: If the prediction is wrong, we can narrow down the failed step.
elements.sent-2411-840: Generating explanations acts as a type of regularization method.
elements.sent-2411-841: It results in better answer prediction as the model focuses on proper reasoning instead of taking shortcuts.
elements.figure-2411-031: \includegraphics[width=8cm, height=6cm, keepaspectratio]{explain-1.PNG} \caption{Example of both visual explanation and textual explanation. Figure from \cite{faithfulexplanation}}
elements.sent-2411-842: Visual explanations are attention heatmaps indicating which image regions the model focused on for answer prediction.
elements.sent-2411-843: Textual explanations are natural language explanations usually comprising of multiple sentences which describe key information the model used in its reasoning process.
elements.sent-2411-844: Earlier explanation models only produced visual explanations as attention heatmaps are easy to build using gradient based methods such as Grad-CAM\cite{gradcam .}
elements.sent-2411-845: Visual explanations are intuitive and reveal the areas of the image the model concentrated on.
elements.sent-2411-846: If the model deduced the right answer but did not look at the right area, the model's reasoning is suspect.
elements.sent-2411-847: If the model failed to answer correctly, we can analyse the attention map to figure out why the model failed.
elements.sent-2411-848: But visual explanations alone may not be enough to fully understand how the model is operating.
elements.sent-2411-849: Many times the model attends to the correct region but still fails to use the visual information correctly in its inference process.
elements.sent-2411-850: Textual explanations can provide better insights and reveal whether the attended visual information was used correctly.
elements.sent-2411-851: Natural language explanations are also easier to understand for humans.
elements.sent-2411-852: Textual explanations can explain the reasoning process step by step which is clearer than a single attention map.
elements.sent-2411-853: The best approach seems to be producing both types of explanations.
elements.sent-2411-854: Models generating such multimodal explanations argue that visual and textual explanations complement each-other.
elements.sent-2411-855: Both fine-tune each other and improve each other's quality.
elements.sent-2411-856: Generating natural language explanations results in better attention maps making them less noisy, more relevant and focused.
elements.sent-2411-857: On the other hand, attending to important image regions motivates the textual explanations to be more relevant, more succinct and more related to image content.
elements.sent-2411-858: It devises a way to automatically collect this attention supervision from available region descriptions and object annotations.
elements.sent-2411-859: In the “explaining” part, the image is used to detect attributes and produce a caption.
elements.sent-2411-860: These are used in the “reasoning” part in place of the image to infer the correct answer.
elements.sent-2411-861: As the model bases its reasoning on the produced caption and attributes, they can be thought of as textual explanations explaining what information the model deduced its answer from.
elements.sent-2411-862: It argues that forcing the model to produce natural language explanations can help it predict more accurate answers.
elements.sent-2411-863: It then uses this structured information about the relevant scene entities to produce suitable natural language explanations.
elements.sent-2411-864: Prominent objects in the attention map should be mentioned in the explanation text and the textual explanation should ground any object it mentions in the image.
elements.sent-2411-865: In this way, both explanations calibrate each-other resulting in less noise and more relevant explanations.
elements.sent-2411-866: Graph models use graphs to process information from the image and the question.
elements.sent-2411-867: Graph models argue that conventional VQA models cannot do complex reasoning with their monolithic vector representations.
elements.sent-2411-868: Graph models pin the ability of a model to do higher-level reasoning on its capacity to perform relational reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-869: Almost any complex question about an image requires reasoning about its objects and relationships.
elements.sent-2411-870: For example, a ‘boy’ and a ‘sandwich’ with the relation ‘eating’ encodes an important fact about an image.
elements.sent-2411-871: Another fact like this could be (‘boy’, ‘next to’, ‘dog’).
elements.sent-2411-872: Now if a VQA model is asked the question ‘What is the boy next to the dog eating?’, it will be much more successful in answering the question if it understands the various relationships that exist among the multiple objects.
elements.sent-2411-873: As we will see, graph models use graphs to model these relationships and use them to answer questions.
elements.sent-2411-874: Intra-modality graphs are graphs belonging to a single modality, either the image or the question.
elements.sent-2411-875: In image graphs, the nodes represent the objects detected in the image and the edges represent the relationships between them.
elements.sent-2411-876: In question graphs, the nodes represent the words and the edges could represent their syntactic dependencies or some other relationship.
elements.sent-2411-877: Having two separate graphs means that the image and the question do not interact with each other but rather their elements interact within themselves.
elements.sent-2411-878: In this way, intra-modality graphs can model self-attention or simply produce better image and question features.
elements.sent-2411-879: On the other hand, inter-modality graphs model interactions between the two modalities.
elements.sent-2411-880: These interactions could be direct(fusion) or indirect(attention).

elements.sent-2411-881: In the question graph, nodes are word features and edges are syntactic dependency types as predicted by the Stanford Dependency Parser.
elements.sent-2411-882: In the scene graph, nodes are object features and edges are relative spatial positions.
elements.sent-2411-883: LCGN\cite{lcgn and TRRNet\cite{trrnet also use object features as nodes in their graphs.}}
elements.sent-2411-884: While LCGN uses all detected objects, TRRNet selects top k objects using the question.
elements.sent-2411-885: NSM\cite{nsm generates a scene graph where each object node is accompanied by a bounding box, a mask, dense visual features, and a collection of discrete probability distributions for each of the object’s semantic properties, such as its color, material, shape, etc., defined over a fixed concept vocabulary.}
elements.sent-2411-886: Some graph models do not represent objects as nodes.
elements.sent-2411-887: Rather they fuse the object features with the question\cite{cgs}\cite{murel}\cite{regat or the words\cite{bgn and treat the joint embeddings as individual nodes.}}
elements.sent-2411-888: In each iteration, each node gathers context and information from its neighbors and updates itself.
elements.sent-2411-889: The edges could also update alongside the nodes\cite{lcgn but most models only implement node updates.}
elements.sent-2411-890: Each node has its own neighborhood where neighborhood could mean all other nodes or a subset of them based on some criterion.
elements.sent-2411-891: For example, the criterion could be that a node’s neighbors are the top k nodes according to edge weights.
elements.sent-2411-892: The neighborhood selection criterion is important due to computational concerns.
elements.sent-2411-893: A fully connected graph where each node considers all other nodes its neighbors is computation-heavy.
elements.sent-2411-894: On the other hand, a more choosy selection criterion might leave out relevant nodes and miss important relations and interactions.
elements.sent-2411-895: In most graph models like Graph-VQA\cite{graph-vqa and NSM\cite{nsm , each node considers its adjacent nodes to be neighbors.}}
elements.sent-2411-896: In CGS\cite{cgs , a node’s neighbors correspond to the K most similar nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-897: In other graph models like MuReL\cite{murel and LCGN\cite{lcgn , the graph is fully connected and each node considers all other nodes its neighbors.}}
elements.sent-2411-898: We discuss these three methods through three models that each make use of one of them.
elements.sent-2411-900: Graph convolution can be thought to summarize information from a particular neighborhood of nodes.
elements.sent-2411-901: Multiple layers of convolution can produce higher and higher levels of representations of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-902: CGS implements a patch operator by using a set of K Gaussian kernels of learnable means and covariances.
elements.sent-2411-903: For a given vertex, the output of the patch operator can be thought of as a weighted sum of the neighboring features.
elements.sent-2411-905: In the final step of processing, it uses self-attention to produce attention weights that are used to compute the final features.
elements.sent-2411-906: ReGAT argues that compared to graph convolutional networks which treat all neighbors the same, graph attention assigns different weights to different neighbors and this can reflect which relations are more relevant to a specific question.
elements.sent-2411-907: Finally, we have graph traversal which is used by NSM\cite{nsm .}
elements.sent-2411-908: NSM converts the question into a set of instructions and treats the graph as a state machine where the nodes correspond to states and the edges correspond to transitions.
elements.sent-2411-909: Then it performs iterative computations where the question-generated instructions are treated as input and traversing the graph simulates traversing the state machine’s states effectively performing sequential reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-910: At each instruction step, NSM traverses the graph by using the current nodes and edges to determine the next set of nodes.
elements.sent-2411-911: Node features are not updated in graph traversal.
elements.sent-2411-912: Traversal just determines the final set of nodes the answer will be based on.
elements.sent-2411-913: After processing, some models\cite{cgs}\cite{murel}\cite{graph-vqa summarize the node features in some way to represent the entire final graph.}
elements.sent-2411-914: The summarization can be done by max-pooling or averaging the top k nodes or by predicting attention weights on the final nodes or some other method.
elements.sent-2411-915: This final representation is then used with the question to predict the answer.

elements.sent-2411-916: Many recent VQA models have utilized BERT-like transformer models to extract cross-modal joint embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-917: BERT\cite{bert is a transformer based model that is pre-trained on several proxy tasks to learn better linguistic representations.}
elements.sent-2411-918: Inspired by BERT's success on a wide range of NLP benchmarks, recent efforts have tried to leverage task-agnostic pre-training to tackle Vision-and-Language(V+L) challenges.
elements.sent-2411-919: Most V+L tasks rely on joint multimodel embeddings to bridge the semantic gap between the visual and textual elements in image and text.
elements.sent-2411-920: Tranformer models try to learn this joint representation through pre-training similar to BERT.
elements.sent-2411-921: Instead of using separate vision and language pre-training, unified multi-modal pre-training allows the model to implicitly discover useful alignments between both sets of inputs and build up a useful joint representation.
elements.sent-2411-922: BERT-like models define some proxy tasks to enable this pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-923: The tasks involve masking one part of the data and training the model to predict the ‘missing’ part from the context provided by the remaining data.
elements.sent-2411-924: Through these proxy tasks, the model gains a deeper level of semantic understanding.
elements.sent-2411-925: In the two-stream architecture, two seperate transformers are applied to image and text and their outputs are fused by a third Transformer in a later stage.
elements.sent-2411-926: In contrast, single-stream models use a single transformer for joint intra-modal and inter-modal interaction.
elements.sent-2411-927: ViLBERT\cite{vilbert , LXMERT\cite{lxmert , and ERNIE-ViL\cite{ernie-vil use the two-stream architecture while VisualBERT\cite{visualbert , VL-BERT\cite{vl-bert , UNITER\cite{uniter , VLP\cite{vlp , Oscar\cite{oscar , and Pixel-BERT\cite{pixel-bert use the single-stream method.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-928: Two-stream models argue that using separate transformers to perform isolated self-attention brings more specific representations while single-stream models argue that self-attention benefits more from the context provided by the other modality resulting in better cross-modal alignment.
elements.sent-2411-929: The proxy tasks must force the model to use information from one modality to make valid inferences about the other.
elements.sent-2411-930: In the process, the model is expected to mine useful cross-modal representations that encode general semantic information.
elements.sent-2411-931: We describe some of these tasks.
elements.sent-2411-932: In MLM(Masked Language Modelling), some textual input tokens are randomly masked and the model has to figure out these tokens by using information from the remaining textual and visual tokens.
elements.sent-2411-933: Only using textual information often results in ambiguity which can only be solved by utilising image information.
elements.sent-2411-934: This forces the model to learn useful alignments between the modalities.
elements.sent-2411-935: By definition, MLM provides bi-directional context where all textual tokens that were not selected for masking are visible.
elements.sent-2411-936: VLP\cite{vlp introduces an additional task in the form of unidirectional MLM where only the tokens on the left of the ‘to-be-predicted’ token are visible to the model.}
elements.sent-2411-937: In MRFR(Masked Region Feature Regression), the model has to regress region features of randomly masked image regions.
elements.sent-2411-938: This task is harder to train on because regressing on a pixel level is difficult and visual ambiguity is more abundant than language ambiguity.
elements.sent-2411-939: For example, there are endless variations of valid pixel arrangements for a region labeled ‘dog’.
elements.sent-2411-940: MRC(Masked Region Classification) is a more tenable task where the labels of randomly selected image regions are masked and the model has to predict the missing object labels.
elements.sent-2411-941: UNITER\cite{uniter introduces the WRA(Word-Region Alignment) task where Optimal transport is used to explicitly encourage fine-grained alignment between words and image regions.}
elements.sent-2411-942: Optimal transport tries to minimize the cost of transporting the embeddings from image regions to words in a sentence and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-943: In addition to text and image, Oscar\cite{oscar provides a third input in the form of object tags.}
elements.sent-2411-944: Oscar argues that object tags can act as helpful anchors to ease the learning of cross-modal alignments.
elements.sent-2411-945: Oscar defines a contrastive loss task where the model has to match objects tags to valid images.
elements.sent-2411-946: ERNIE-ViL\cite{ernie-vil introduces scene graph prediction where the model is provided with three tasks - object prediction, attribute prediction, and relationship prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-947: ERNIE-ViL contends that MLM pre-training alone cannot help the model determine whether a word represents an object or an attribute or a relation and that scene-graphs have to be explicitly included in the training objective for the model to gain better understanding.
elements.sent-2411-948: Pixel-BERT\cite{pixel-bert argues that using region features from pre-trained object detectors causes an information gap because useful information like shape, spatial position, scene information, etc., are lost.}
elements.sent-2411-949: Instead of region features, Pixel-BERT uses raw pixels as the visual input.
elements.sent-2411-950: To make this manageable, a random sampling method is used.
elements.sent-2411-951: This makes training computationally feasible and encourages the model to learn semantic knowledge from incomplete visual input which enhances robustness.. \par.
elements.sent-2411-952: Many questions about images relate to text present in the image.
elements.sent-2411-953: For example, “What does the sign say?”, “What time does the clock show?”, etc. Questions like this require new answers unseen during training.
elements.sent-2411-954: As it is not possible to include all possible answers in the test set, the model needs to have the ability to generate novel answers.
elements.sent-2411-955: Most of the existing VQA models are inept at reasoning about scene text.
elements.sent-2411-956: But some recent models have tried to tackle this problem.
elements.sent-2411-957: LoRRA\cite{textvqa is a VQA model which has an additional OCR module that allows it to answer questions about scene text.}
elements.sent-2411-958: The OCR module extracts all texts in the image as tokens and LoRRA determines which of these tokens is needed in the answering process.
elements.sent-2411-959: It can also output one of the tokens as the answer.
elements.sent-2411-960: ST-VQA\cite{stvqa is another model that tries to incorporate scene text understanding.}
elements.sent-2411-961: ST-VQA argues that PHOC\cite{phoc should be used for embedding the extracted text instead of traditional word embedding models.}
elements.sent-2411-962: Unlike other word embedding models, PHOC puts more emphasis on the morphology of words rather than the semantics.
elements.sent-2411-963: As a result, it is more suitable for representing out-of-vocabulary words such as, license plate numbers.
elements.sent-2411-964: Among the various sub-problems of VQA, counting is often singled out because of it's difficulty.
elements.sent-2411-965: Models that use CNNs for image features are prone to failing on counting questions.
elements.sent-2411-966: This is because the convolution and pooling layers of CNN aggregate spatial information of various objects together.
elements.sent-2411-967: The resulting features can identify the presence of a certain object but cannot count how many instances of that object are there.
elements.sent-2411-968: So, more recent models have switched to using object detectors.
elements.sent-2411-969: Object detectors can detect all instances of a particular object.
elements.sent-2411-970: The only issue here is that the predicted bounding boxes sometimes overlap which can lead to duplicate counting.
elements.sent-2411-971: Another issue is the information loss that occurs in the attention layer.
elements.sent-2411-972: Almost all models use normalized soft attention which sums up the attention weights to equal $1.0$.
elements.sent-2411-973: So if an image contains $1$ cat, that cat will receive an attention weight of $1.0$ but if there are $2$ cats both will receive weights of $0.5$ which effectively means that the two cats are being averaged back into a single cat.
elements.sent-2411-974: So, instead of all instances of the object getting the highest attention, attention gets divided and diluted.
elements.sent-2411-975: Skipping normalization is not an option as \cite{tipsandtricks shows it degrades performance on non-counting questions while the increase in accuracy for counting questions is not that significant.}
elements.sent-2411-976: IRLC uses reinforcement learning to train a scoring function based on the question.
elements.sent-2411-977: The Object with the highest score gets selected and this selection prompts new scores for the remaining objects.
elements.sent-2411-978: But the use of reinforcement learning hinders assimilating this method in existing VQA models.
elements.sent-2411-979: Counter\cite{counter is a model that uses object detection to construct a graph of all detected objects based on how they overlap.}
elements.sent-2411-980: Edges in the graph are removed based on several heuristics to ensure that each object is only counted once.
elements.sent-2411-981: RCN\cite{tallyqa is a model made specifically for the VQA counting task.}
elements.sent-2411-982: It uses Faster R-CNN to detect foreground(fg) and background(bg) objects.
elements.sent-2411-983: It then performs relational reasoning with all fg-fg and fg-bg object pairs.
elements.sent-2411-984: RCN argues that using relational reasoning instead of directly working with the bounding boxes allows it to overcome the overlapping issue.
elements.sent-2411-985: Dataset bias has been a persistent issue in VQA from the start.
elements.sent-2411-986: Many papers\cite{analysis1}\cite{analysis2}\cite{vqa-v2}\cite{clevr}\cite{yinyang}\cite{gvqa have produced findings that suggest that most of the existing models in VQA increase their performance by utilising spurious correlations such as, statistical biases and language priors instead of properly reasoning about the image and question.}
elements.sent-2411-987: Question bias in particular seems to plague most models making them indifferent to image information.
elements.sent-2411-988: Existing VQA datasets contain extremely skewed question and answer distributions.
elements.sent-2411-989: So it's hard to distinguish whether a model is really doing well or if it's just using these biases.
elements.sent-2411-990: To diagnose bias-reliance in models, VQA-cp\cite{vqacp was introduced.}
elements.sent-2411-991: It re-organizes the VQA dataset(both v1 and v2) to make the training and test splits contain different distributions.
elements.sent-2411-992: So only models with actual generalization ability do well on the test set while biased models fail.
elements.sent-2411-993: There have been many recent efforts at bias reduction in VQA.
elements.sent-2411-994: Approaches fall roughly under three categories: 1) Utilizing a bias-only model with the main VQA model; 2) Forcing the model to take the image into consideration; and 3) Augmenting new counter-factual samples.
elements.sent-2411-995: The first two approaches try to remove bias from the model side while the third tries to remove bias from the dataset side.
elements.sent-2411-996: The main VQA network tries to modify its question embedding so that it becomes less useful to the question-only model.
elements.sent-2411-997: This results in the question encoder capturing less bias in it's encoding.
elements.sent-2411-998: They argue that the ensembled model learns to use the unbiased model for questions that the biased model fails to answer.
elements.sent-2411-999: HINT\cite{hint argues that the model may focus on the right region in the attention stage but still disregard the visual information from it's reasoning process.}
elements.sent-2411-1000: HINT works on a gradient level to make sure the answering process is affected by the visual branch of the model.
elements.sent-2411-1001: HINT proposes a ranking loss which tries to align it's visual attention with human provided attention annotations.
elements.sent-2411-1002: In contrast, SCR\cite{scr introduces a self-critical training objective which penalizes the model if correct answers are more sensitive to non-important regions than to important regions, and if incorrect answers are more sensitive to important regions than correct answers.}
elements.sent-2411-1003: These samples help the model to locate the underlying causal relations that lead to the correct answer.
elements.sent-2411-1004: A counter-factual sample is produced by slightly modifying the original sample which causes a subtle semantic shift resulting in a different answer.
elements.sent-2411-1005: Take for example an image of a dog.
elements.sent-2411-1006: If the dog in the image is erased or masked, the answer to the question “Is there a dog in the image?” changes from “yes” to “no”.
elements.sent-2411-1007: Usually counter-factual samples differ from the original sample in a slight way while still remaining very similar.
elements.sent-2411-1008: This forces the model to gain a more nuanced understanding in order to differentiate between the two samples.
elements.sent-2411-1009: For VQA, counter-factual samples are produced by modifying the image or the question or both.
elements.sent-2411-1010: Images can be modified by masking one or more regions.
elements.sent-2411-1011: Masking can be done either by cropping out the region\cite{contrast}\cite{cf-gs}\cite{css or by replacing it with GAN-based in-painting\cite{inv-cov}\cite{mutant .}}
elements.sent-2411-1012: Similar to images, questions can be modified by masking one or more words\cite{contrast}\cite{css}\cite{mutant .}
elements.sent-2411-1013: In addition to word masking, \cite{mutant also replaces words with their negations and adversarial substitutes.}
elements.sent-2411-1014: Notice that we did not talk at length about the details of any particular dataset or model.
elements.sent-2411-1015: Our goal here was to give a bird's-eye view of the entire VQA field.
elements.sent-2411-1016: So we chose not to engage in lengthy discussions of any model or dataset.
elements.sent-2411-1017: In preparing this survey, we found that many outdated models still provided valuable insights or a novel way of approaching the problem.
elements.sent-2411-1018: We stress that accuracy is not everything as biases in widely used datasets, VQA-v1 and v2 for example, can present a skewed depiction of a model's true ability.
elements.sent-2411-1019: It would not be a stretch to say that the VQA field has exploded in a few short years.
elements.sent-2411-1020: It is easy to feel overwhelmed and lost as a newcomer.
elements.sent-2411-1021: We hope that our work will provide anyone new to this field with a map and a starting point.. \bibliographystyle{plain \EOD.}
